close-encounters-of-the-third-kind.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind

details.description: After an encounter with UFOs, an electricity linesman feels undeniably drawn to an isolated area in the wilderness where something spectacular is about to happen.

entries.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Close Encounters of the Third Kind

details.description: A young couple travels to a remote island to eat at an exclusive restaurant where the chef has prepared a lavish menu, with some shocking surprises.

entries.title: The Menu
entries.reviewText: That cheeseburger looked really good

entries.title: The Menu
entries.reviewText: The Menu is Outstanding.

details.description: Less than 24 hours into his parole, charismatic thief Danny Ocean is already rolling out his next plan: In one night, Danny's hand-picked crew of specialists will attempt to steal more than $150 million from three Las Vegas casinos. But to score the cash, Danny risks his chances of reconciling with ex-wife, Tess.

austin-powers-international-man-of-myste.title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery

details.description: As a swinging fashion photographer by day and a groovy British superagent by night, Austin Powers is the '60s' most shagadelic spy. But can he stop megalomaniac Dr. Evil after the bald villain freezes himself and unthaws in the '90s? With the help of sexy sidekick Vanessa Kensington, he just might.

entries.title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery
entries.reviewText: Austin Powers is Not Good <3

entries.title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery
entries.reviewText: Austin Powers is Good.

entries.title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery
entries.reviewText: As with other movie at this rating, there are good things to be mentioned. The henchman bits and father-son dynamics are very entertaining. There are solid jokes throughout. However, falls too easily in line with the tropes it seems to want to parody. And at face value, the main story and characters are cringe-worthy.

details.description: A knight framed for a tragic crime teams with a scrappy, shape-shifting teen to prove his innocence.

Kyuta, a boy living in Shibuya, and Kumatetsu, a lonesome beast from Jutengai, an imaginary world. One day, Kyuta forays into the imaginary world and, as he's looking for his way back, meets Kumatetsu who becomes his spirit guide. That encounter leads them to many adventures.

Science teacher Ryland Grace wakes up on a spaceship light years from home with no recollection of who he is or how he got there. As his memory returns, he begins to uncover his mission: solve the riddle of the mysterious substance causing the sun to die out. He must call on his scientific knowledge and unorthodox ideas to save everything on Earth from extinction… but an unexpected friendship means he may not have to do it alone.

entries.title: Project Hail Mary
entries.reviewText: Okay Ryan Gosling and War Crimes Woman and the beauty of interstellar friendship got me

entries.title: Project Hail Mary
entries.reviewText: Saw it again in regular 70mm, and it's still a masterpiece.

entries.title: Project Hail Mary
entries.reviewText: Project Hail Mary is a Masterpiece. A visually stunning, obligatory "IMAX 70MM is beautiful", space odyssey powered by real emotion, sharp wit, and one of Ryan Gosling’s best performances, it somehow makes hard science, huge spectacle, and genuine heart feel perfectly weightless together. Elevating it even further is Rocky, who quickly becomes the soul of the film. He is funny, endearing, and unexpectedly moving for a rock, turning the story into a heartfelt buddy comedy instead of just another survival thriller. The score and OST are the final piece that pushes it over the top, giving the entire film a sense of awe that lingers long after it ends. Ryan Gosling’s performance, Rocky’s practical-screen presence, the story, the script, the score and ost, the practical and special effects, the cinematography, IMAX 70MM! Project Hail Mary is one of those rare films, and rare-er sci-fi films that completely satisfies, thrills the brain, crushes the heart, and leaves you grateful it exists.

entries.title: Project Hail Mary
entries.reviewText: Beautifully cinematic and emotionally moving. Such a good story--I can only imagine it is a faithful adaptation--the characters shine like stars.

entries.title: Project Hail Mary
entries.reviewText: When I say I felt like I went through the complete hero's journey through this film, I mean it. It felt like I went through an entire movie about cats having multiple lives, reincarnation, nirvana, acceptance, the whole nine yards - even if this film had almost none of that. I quite enjoyed this film, and I really wasn't expecting to enjoy it as much as I am at this present point in time.This film, felt complete to me. I had a satisfying start, felt compelled through each plot point, each flashback actually felt relevant, and the conclusion was satisfying. It was environmentally stunning - wonderful shots, beautiful visuals, immersive soundtrack, the whole experience just felt like I was one with the universe. It doesn't try and go too far out there to create complications in the plot, instead relying on a lot of core emotions and experiences to understand why Grace and Rocky bonding felt so... human (for a lack of a better word). I honestly am at a lack of words about how happy I was seeing that film, I'll inevitably edit this review to write more later - so stay tuned.Re joke segment: Ryan Gosling looked so fucking hot in the film. Something about a frumpy nerd with glasses, messy hair, scruffy beard, and watching him bond with Rocky kinda made my heart flutter a little, not gonna lie. This is the secret romance film that we should be getting more often, and it's wrapped in the neat little bow-tie of a friendship - see film makers, we don't need everything to feel carnally driven (just sometimes). Nerdy teachers with messy hair, scruffy beards, and needing a hug - hit me up.

the-lord-of-the-rings-the-return-of-the-.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King

details.description: As armies mass for a final battle that will decide the fate of the world--and powerful, ancient forces of Light and Dark compete to determine the outcome--one member of the Fellowship of the Ring is revealed as the noble heir to the throne of the Kings of Men. Yet, the sole hope for triumph over evil lies with a brave hobbit, Frodo, who, accompanied by his loyal friend Sam and the hideous, wretched Gollum, ventures deep into the very dark heart of Mordor on his seemingly impossible quest to destroy the Ring of Power.​

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
entries.reviewText: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King is a Masterpiece. Visually breathtaking and emotionally powerful, The Return of the King delivers a moving and deeply satisfying conclusion to what still stands as the greatest fantasy trilogy ever put to film. From its opening moments to its final goodbyes, this is a movie operating at the absolute peak of its craft.The acting across the board is extraordinary, with every major performance landing exactly where it needs to. Elijah Wood carries the crushing weight of Frodo’s journey with sincerity. Viggo Mortensen fully embodies Aragorn’s rise into kingship, projecting strength and earned authority, while Ian McKellen’s Gandalf remains the moral and emotional anchor of the story. Even supporting roles are treated with care and respect, giving every character a sense of purpose and payoff.Howard Shore’s score is nothing short of a masterpiece itself. The music does not simply accompany the film, it defines it. Themes introduced throughout the trilogy return with greater emotional weight, evolving alongside the characters and elevating every victory, loss, and moment of sacrifice. Few film scores have ever been so inseparable from the story they are telling.On a technical level, the film is flawless. The scale of the battles is immense, yet they never lose clarity or emotional grounding. Practical effects, miniatures, and early CGI are blended so seamlessly that the world feels real rather than digitally constructed. Peter Jackson balances spectacle with intimacy, ensuring that even the largest sequences never lose sight of the personal stakes at their core.Most importantly, The Return of the King earns its emotional payoff. Every arc, every sacrifice, and every farewell feels justified by the journey that came before it. It is rare for a finale to honor its story, characters, and audience so completely, but this film does exactly that. It is not just the end of a trilogy, it is the culmination of one of cinema’s most ambitious and successful storytelling achievements.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
entries.reviewText: Stunning. Beautiful. A story for the ages. (Though I think the half point off is meant to be for sexism... again...)

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
entries.reviewText: In western lands beneath the Sunthe flowers may rise in Spring,the trees may bud, the waters run,the merry finches sing.Or there maybe 'tis cloudless nightand swaying beeches bearthe Elven-stars as jewels whiteamid their branching hair.Though here at journey's end I liein darkness buried deep,beyond all towers strong and high,beyond all mountains steep,above all shadows rides the Sunand Stars for ever dwell:I will not say the Day is done,nor bid the Stars farewell.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
entries.reviewText: If you have not read my review of the first movie, I would go read that one first. It explains a lot of my general sentiments towards the series in general, as well as my frame of reference. This review will be focused much more on the movie itself as a stand-alone product.Return of the King is perhaps my favorite of the bunch, even if I do think it is not as good as a singular artistic piece than the Fellowship of the Rings (which is pretty unmatched in this regards). It is the culmination of the Lord of the Rings trilogy, wrapping up a rather lengthy tale of mortal peril and demonic stakes into a nice bow-tie extolling the virtues of continued perseverance and unity for good. While it does suffer from an issue I had with the second film (namely it being dependent on another film to have some of the background necessary to understand the film completely), I personally think the film hits its stride in terms of cinematic action and pressing in the dire straights the world is in, leading to a very satisfying movie experience.As mentioned in my review of Fellowship of the Rings, I think this trilogy is such a good flagship example of fantasy as a genre, and these particular films help exemplify this through good storytelling, environmental pacing, and simplistic (but relatable) character arc. If the first film is the best complete product, then this film is the best for tying up loose ends and providing a satisfying ending. Much like how each film ends its tale by extolling the virtues of believing in innate good and perseverance through trials and tribulations, Return of the King gets the pleasure of stamping each character's journey with a wax seal of joy - though this time, more permanently fixed. Each character's growth and suffering finally bears fruit as the White Tree blossoms once again and Sauron is defeated for good, with bonus scenes of Frodo and Gandalf's departure as a metaphor for death / letting go. Aragorn and Frodo are the biggest beneficiaries of this, since they have been the main driving forces of the plot from the start, but I would not count out the other hobbits just yet. Pippin FINALLY gets a redemption arc (kinda) worth being happy about and Sam absolutely carries the last hour or so of the film in terms of emotional digging. Even Gimli and Legolas get more chummy with each other, really rounding out how even as paths weave and stray, everyone has enough strength to carry themselves through troubling times. This is where I applaud Jackson's decision not to include the Scouring of the Shire in the film, despite its massive importance in the books. The film itself has such a strong narrative thread leading up to Aragorn's coronation that ending the film on a downer, despite the overall theme that good must be persistent in the face of evil, I think would take away from the film's message while not adding more of note. Had the film taken different narrative choices (focusing more on the idealistic dream of the Shire's return on Sam for example), maaaybe it could have worked, but the film was already long enough as is.While I do have some minor gripes with the film, such as the Eowyn-Aragorn pining subplot, overall, I am so happy with the film's conclusion I am going to choose to ignore venting about all of that. I am just so thrilled to see an emotionally satisfying ending (just ignore the fact this journey took over multiple years to complete and not magically in a year or so like its presented in the films)! This film also has probably the largest concentration of bearded men I love, thank you Rohan and Gondor <3. Eomer's the hottest man para mi, but Faramir screams hubby material to me (sorry Eowyn). Aragorn would be right up there, but I know I'd never compare to Arwen. There's just something about a hot, bearded man on horseback fighting to save the world that gets my heart pacing I suppose (probably helps they aren't written as complete idiots or rude). Teach me how to ride a stallion I guess.Anyways, for concluding marks, this film gives me the satisfying conclusion to this 10+ hour long story that I so desperately craved. While some points did cause me to groan (much of the Hobbit's journey is very subtle in emotion and caused me much frustration for Sam bc he was absolutely carrying Frodo's poor ass), I do appreciate both how the power of man isn't supremely exaggerated through the guise of magic and the central theme of core values and hope shine bright like the Phial of Galadriel. This is a good example of a film that despite lots of fighting, dreariness, and emotional turmoil, doesn't feel like the narration gets lost in the bravado of trying to intentionally create climatic moments that are such obvious buildups it ruins the immersion. Lord of the Rings as a whole is a film franchise I believe is worth a watch, and there is simply much more to discover in this realm - I just don't know if we'll ever get something as fulfilling like this again.

the-lord-of-the-rings-the-fellowship-of-.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring

details.description: Young hobbit Frodo Baggins, after inheriting a mysterious ring from his uncle Bilbo, must leave his home in order to keep it from falling into the hands of its evil creator. Along the way, a fellowship is formed to protect the ringbearer and make sure that the ring arrives at its final destination: Mt. Doom, the only place where it can be destroyed.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
entries.reviewText: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring is Outstanding. The acting across the board is phenomenal, anchored by Elijah Wood’s earnest Frodo and Ian McKellen’s iconic Gandalf. Viggo Mortensen instantly defines Aragorn as both mythic and mysterious, while Sean Astin grounds the entire journey with Sam’s sincerity. Cate Blanchett’s Galadriel is ethereal, and the literally everyone else in this film consistently elevates even the smallest moments. Howard Shore’s score is timeless, weaving so many motifs together so seamlessly that the music feels inseparable from the world itself. From the Shire’s warmth to the looming menace of Mordor, the score does as much narrative work as the dialogue. The story is largely excellent, successfully adapting the immense and beloved text while establishing a rich lived-in world with a clear sense of stakes.My main issues with Fellowship are twofold: pacing and action cinematography. In terms of pacing, the film is constantly hopping from place to place, rarely lingering long enough to fully explore any location before moving on. This creates a quantity over quality effect where the breadth of Middle-earth comes at the expense of deeper immersion in individual settings. While this is largely a consequence of the source material, and likely not something Peter Jackson could have fully solved, it still slightly detracts from the film’s overall impact. However, more frustrating is the action cinematography. For a film that achieves such a high level of craftsmanship elsewhere, the action scenes feel bafflingly amateurish, relying heavily on jump cuts and speed ramping to obscure choreography and spatial clarity. Instead of smooth, deliberate action that lets the audience track movement and intent, these moments blur together into amorphous sequences where impact is implied rather than felt. It is a glaring weakness in an otherwise monumental achievement, made only worse because so much else is executed so well.Overall, The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring is Outstanding with its strengths largely defining the experience. Its performances, music, and worldbuilding establish a cinematic beast that no other film (outside of the sequels) has managed to outdo. While its pacing and muddled action cinematography occasionally hold it back from true perfection, those flaws never diminish the sense of wonder, sincerity, and ambition at its core. Fellowship succeeds as both the beginning of an epic journey and a landmark in blockbuster filmmaking, one whose emotional weight and craftsmanship continue to endure.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
entries.reviewText: And then they walk, walk, walk, walk and we love them for it.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
entries.reviewText: IAN: When I suggested to Sean that he took Elijah’s hand it was because I thought anyone who knew the book would care about the deep friendship, often of an innocently physical nature, and that might’ve been missed by two resolutely heterosexual actors who mightn’t appreciate that gay people like myself saw in a touch something perhaps more meaningful than others might. So to persuade him to touch Elijah, I’d say, “Well look, it’s in the book.”[Sean & Elijah in a separate recording]SEAN: Ian brought the book to me right before we shot it and he said, “Now look here, it says that Sam runs over and grabs Frodo’s hand,” he said. “The fans of the book are going to want to see that.” I sort of—I believed it, and I got a fan letter the other day that a neighbor friend handed to me, and it said how much it meant to her that Sam holds Frodo’s hand at that moment because it was something that she—it was one of the most important moments to her in the book.ELIJAH: Oh, that’s fantastic. It’s those subtle little nuances, man.SEAN: So thank you Ian.ELIJAH: That’s unbelievable.

entries.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
entries.reviewText: Lord of the Rings as a whole is a film franchise I find very fascinating, visually appealing, and overall a compelling story. I will preface this review in that I have not read the books but I am somewhat aware of details in the books not in the movies and vice versa, though they don't really affect my viewing pleasure (minus a few plot holes, but they're not enough to detract any points from me).The first of three, this movie is perhaps the most full-circle of the trilogy in that it feels like the most complete story with a beginning and an end that doesn't feel like it's leading to something bigger that's purposely omitted from your viewing experience and detracting from it consequently. From where the film ends it obviously preludes to the bigger struggle that the Fellowship will take in the subsequent two films, but it feels the most like a neatly-wrapped bow tie on a very pleasant coming-of-age story. While I will admit it is not my favorite of the three (that belongs to Return of the King), I will admit it has the easiest barrier of entry and the best flow of story. This is its greatest strength: there is an obvious growth of progression as the Hobbits slowly realize the gravitas of the situation they are in, the crew starts off idealistic and slowly become hardened over time facing challenges, and they all face challenges that they work to overcome in order to progress the story. These themes continue throughout the next several films, but this particular one has the advantage it has the most amount of "innocence" amongst the crew. While I was cringing at the stupidity of some decisions made (Gandalf's remarks towards Pippin for example), it is a good example of both highlighting the starkness of awareness characters have as well as showing the audience how deep they still have to go. This in turn helps the first film be one of the stronger self-contained films of the bunch.On a different note, the visuals of the film are spectacular for its time. While watching it on a modern screen has shown some aging (especially noticeable in the CGI used in architecture), it is a very beautiful movie, both visually and sonically. The film has the advantage of it utilizing very common fantasy tropes to drive its artistic direction, but it does it in a very beautiful fashion because it allows nature to shine through. Intentional with the story itself, since magic does not take a dramatic spotlight, the visuals compliment this by never feeling like an eyesore. The music choice also serves to compliment this, twinging at common sounds (ex: elvish singing) to really hammer in the fantasy feel without anything ever feeling overbearing. It leaves everything with a rather pretty finish, never feeling unnecessary and directly supplementing the experience. It has subtle framing to help further the film's theme of it being a coming-of-age story by letting narrative choices shine.Speaking of which, Lord of the Rings as a whole has really strong narrative choices, part in due to the source material. While yes, Tolkien absolutely did lift much of his mythos from other sources, and yes, the parallels the conflicts in this story present can be dissected back towards the author's biases, and yes, I don't think anything this film franchise (or book) would present would pass the Bechdel test, it has genuinely compelling storytelling. Tolkien was able to world-build a truly expansive world (fueled by his linguistic addiction no doubt) while simultaneously leaving a simple story to follow if you boil it down to its core components. The first film has the advantage the crew is together 99% of the time, so there's no need to pay attention to split parties, but this in turn lends itself back to the whole nature of this section being a coming-of-age story. We'll cover this more in detail in subsequent film reviews, but for the first film, its easy to feel Frodo's realizations about the Ring or the struggles surrounding conflicts in this universe fueled by the Ring. The down-to-Earth, depressing conflicts help to drive the reality of the film despite its fantasy nature. The compelling towards evil (minus not explaining Saruman's descent in more detail) is a theme that is explored (and will continue to be explored) helps to drive in Tolkien's views towards good and evil in a philosophical sense. The movie aims to display this not necessarily through a black-and-white sense, choosing to display it through a moralistic struggle pulled in many directions towards compelling forces and struggles. While yes, there is an absolutely obvious evil (Sauron, Saruman, Orcs), the whole franchise drives in the point that perfection is a struggle even Elves fail at. A rather bleak take on the world, the film dampers traditionally idealistic takes on fantasy while also showing growth and struggles everyone must take.Overall, I think this film is a flagship film for the traditional fantasy genre and has withstood the test of time brilliantly. It presents a lot of information but never feels like it's getting bogged down in its own presentation, leading to very clean storytelling that makes for a very engaging watching experience. The actors are also all good and don't detract from the viewing experience (major props to Christopher Lee and Patrick Stewart), so kudos to this entire cast and crew.For the joke segment, this film gets a 11/10 for me for the amount of hot bearded men in the entire series that I can oggle over and objectify, but even then, this film would be solid without it.

details.description: Presenting the tale of American founding father Alexander Hamilton, this filmed version of the original Broadway smash hit is the story of America then, told by America now.

entries.title: Hamilton
entries.reviewText: This movie simply remains a masterpiece.

entries.title: Hamilton
entries.reviewText: Hamilton is a Masterpiece. A revolutionary love letter of Broadway, hip-hop, and American history, it transforms a dense historical narrative into something electrifying, emotional, and endlessly rewatchable; with nearly every lyric carrying a double meaning, either an homage to one of the show's many influences or a subtle setup for something that has already happened or yet to come. Each rewatch reveals new layers, callbacks, and lyrical craftsmanship that deepen the experience. With Lin-Manuel Miranda’s lyrics being razor sharp, the performances are uniformly phenomenal, and the staging is somehow both minimalist and infinitely expressive. More than just a filmed stage show, Hamilton is timeless in its themes of legacy, ambition, and the cost of greatness. It is one of the rare works that fully earns its cultural impact and lives up to every ounce of its reputation.

entries.title: Hamilton
entries.reviewText: Hamilton is a jam-packed musical filled with stunning staging choices, a rather fresh discography (compared to other musicals I feel), and an overall strong execution for what it was going for. It feels like it always has its foot on the gas pedal and never lets up (except for a few moments for additional emotional impact).The nature of a musical-turned-film for the purposes of review falls into two ideas for me: the discography by itself, and the overall experience watching a recording of a live performance.Regarding the first point, I think its important to note that music from musicals tend to have heavier emphasis of lyricism and setting a scene, meaning sometimes that instrumental choices are sacrificed for the sake of driving narrative in a whole story. In Hamilton's particular case, its musical inspirations (very heavy on hip hop, R&B, and soul music) are reflected in its discography (and actor choices as well). I rather appreciate this, it provides a very interesting twist to what is typically thought of on the category of musical numbers - only further enhanced by the cast's fantastic diction and overall ability to perform live. The advantage of being able to listen to the discography at my own leisure means I can pick and choose how I want to listen to the experience, which enhances by appreciation for the songs - which leads me to my second considerations:Regarding the second point, for my viewing experience, I felt that the musical's choice to go full steam ahead left me wanting more breathing room to fully digest all of the moments the musical had to offer. The few moments that did over moments of brevity / drop in instrumentation for a significant period of time were used to either lead into more triumphant moments musically or to allow dramatic emotional turmoil to sink in (ex: Eliza in the latter half of Act 2) were some of my favorite parts of the experience. It is my appreciation of those moments that allowed me a moment to let the gravitas of the matters sink in that I wish I had more time either between pieces or dialogue to help transition timing. While I have been told that the choice to perform 46 songs back-to-back is actually intentional, as a "through composed" musical (similar to opera), it did affect my viewing experience. This is probably why Hamilton works phenomenally as a discography but less-so for me as a musical. Everything develops as the tracks progress through the album - and much like listening to a complete album, multiple listens are preferred to get a better digested experience. It's a bit harder to do that with watching an entire musical versus being able to pause a song at any time, since the musical should be enjoyed in its entirety as a whole art form.On a tangential note, I have nothing but praise for the environmental and visual choices the musical chose. From the rotating stage allowing for very interesting blocking, the costuming changing as the musical progresses to reflect changes in time and fashion choices), and the usage of the camera for the purposes of the movie to give additional point of views we normally would not be able to see in the audience - all of this provided a very compelling experience for a first-time viewer who has never seen this musical before. The cast I felt was absolutely amazing, and the decision to go with a non-white cast for the Founding Fathers as a reflection of the intentional music choices was well-chosen in my opinion. The choice also to have actors play multiple roles in the same performance also allowed them to flex their chops and the musical does enough to allow the audience to distinguish them as separate entities so we don't get confused, which I appreciate. My personal favorite performances out of this cast were Diggs as Lafayette, Soo as Eliza, and Goldsberry as Angelica (honorable mention Groff as King George III), but the entire cast is worthy of standing applause. I will say that while I do not appreciate Miranda's singing, I do appreciate his charisma in performing helps carry an otherwise sub-par singing experience. He does have a certain charm about him that did endear me to his character as the musical went on.Overall, I enjoyed the musical as someone who is new to the genre, and I definitely will consider watching this live if given the opportunity. I also quite enjoy the album, with many songs being particularly enthusing to listen to, and the overall experience when listening to the album from start to finish being a very enjoyable one. My gripes are less-so faults with the musical itself, since the choices they made were done to drive in the punchiness and pace intentionally set by Miranda - they were just not my cup of tea.I can see why this musical became a massive success, a moment of cultural zeitgeist for its time. While I cannot say that I share the same level of fervor, having watched the musical a full decade after its premiere, I will say that this is a musical that I do think is worth watching and a form of art I think has a lot to appreciate.
